question: Slack or Float. The slack is with reference to an event and float is with respect to an activity. In other works, slack is used with PERT and float with CPM, but in general practice, they may be used interchangeably. The slack or float represents the difference between the earliest completion or finish time and the latest allowable time. The slack or float may be positive, negative or zero. When the slack or float of an activity is positive, then it represents a situation where extra resources are available and the completion of project is not delayed. When the slack or float of an activity is negative, then it represents that a programme falls behind schedule and additional resources are required to complete the project in time. When the slack or float of an activity is zero, then the activity is critical and any delay in its performance will delay the completion of whole project. The slack or float may be total float, free float, interfering and independent float. The total float is the time available for an activity performance minus the duration of activity. It may be defined as the difference of the latest finish time (LFT) and earliest finish time (EFT). It is also equal to the difference of latest start time (LST) and earliest start time (EST). The free float is defined as the earliest start time (EST) of its successor activity and the earliest finish time (EFT) of the activity in question. The interfering float is the difference between the total float and free float of an activity. The independent float is the excess of minimum available time over the required activity duration : Notes:	 (a)	 The earliest possible time at which an activity may start is called earliest start time (EST).

options.c: Pessimistic or worst time. It is the time which an activity will take to complete in case of difficulty. It is the longest

These methods are discussed as follows: 16.12  Bar Chart or Gantt Chart The bar chart or Gantt chart was developed by Henry Gantt for planning and scheduling the methods of construction. This chart is a graphical representation of various activities involved in a construction work. It consists of two co- ordinates. The horizontal ordinate or bar represents the various activities involved in a construction work and the duration of times required for completion of the activities. The vertical abscissa or bar represents the respective jobs to be performed. The length of the bar indicates the duration of activity or job for its completion. The various activities are shown parallel to each other along ordinates. Since the various activities are shown by a number of parallel ordinates or bars, therefore it is called a bar chart. The duration of the activities are represented from the left to the right. Each bar is divided longitudinally into two portions. The top portion indicates the progress of completion of the activity and the bottom portion indicates the duration of the activity. Construction Management The use of bar chart is very popular in construction departments because of the following advantages:

options.a: It is very simple to prepare and interpret.
options.b: Each item of work or activity is shown separately.
options.c: It is very easy to compare the progress and original schedule.
options.d: It is easy to know the time for the resource of activities.

question: Non-transit A theodolite is called a transit theodolite, when its telescope can be transited i.e. revolved through a complete revolution about its horizontal axis in a vertical plane. In a non-transist theodolite, the telescope cannot be transited. These types of theodolites have now become obsolete. The theodolites are also classified as vernier theodolites and micrometer theodolites, according as the vernier or micrometer is fitted to read the graduated circle. The first type is more commonly used. The following terms should be well understood when manipulating a transit theodolite: *  The magnetic needle is an essential part of the various forms of compass.

options.d: Axis of telescope. It is a line joining the optical centre of the object glass and the centre of the eye piece.

question: Plain sedimentation. It is the process of retaining water in a basin so that the suspended particles settles down as a result of the action of gravity and forces. The rate of settling of a particle in water depends upon the viscosity of water, density of water, specific gravity of the particle, shape and size of the particle. It may be noted that the particles will settle down more rapidly in water at higher temperatures than at lower temperatures. The process of plain sedimentation may remove 60% suspended matter and 75% bacteria. The following are the important factors to be considered in designing a sedimentation tank:

options.a: Period of detention. It is the time required to fill the tank at the given rate of flow. The detention time for an
options.b: Depth of tank. It is also an important factor and should be 3 metres to 6 metres.
options.c: Velocity of flow. The velocity of flow of water is obtained by dividing the volumetric flow by vertical cross-
options.d: Sludge capacity. The sludge capacity depends upon the period of cleanings and the amount of matter removed

subject: Structural Analysis
question: By balancing back sight and foresight the errors eliminated are

options.a: errors due to non-parallelism of line of collimation and axis of bubble tube

subject: Structural Analysis
question: Balancing depth. When the quantity of excavated earth can be fully utilised by making the banks on both sides of the canal, then the cross-section of the canal is said be economical section or balancing section. The depth of cutting for that ideal condition is known as balancing depth. It is possible only when the canal section is partly in cutting and partly in filling. The cost of earth work will also be balanced. 7.19  Canal Lining The laying of the impervious layer which protects the bed and sides of the canal is called canal lining. The lining of a canal assures economical water distribution, reduces possibility of breaching, prevents silting channel and increases available head for power generation. In a lined canal, the resistance to flow decreases and velocity of flow increases. It also decreases the maintenance of the canal. The lining of a canal is necessary for the following reasons:

options.a: to minimise the seepage losses through the bed and sides of the canal.
options.b: to prevent scouring and erosion of bed and sides of the canal due to high velocity of flood water at the time of
options.c: to increase the discharge in the canal section by increasing the velocity.
options.d: to prevent water logging of area which takes place due to rise in ground water table.

subject: Structural Analysis
question: The term balancing depth has the same meaning as the depth of water in canal.

question: Gradient The rise or fall of the rail track level per unit length of the track is known as gradient. Gradient on railways may be classified as: (i) Ruling gradient (ii) Momentum gradient (iii) Pusher gradient (iv) Station yard gradient (i) Ruling gradient To balance between efficient running of the train as well as to take care of drainage even on plane land suitable gradient should be given. Such gradient is called ruling gradient. In India ruling gradients are: 1 in 150 to 1 in 200 in plains. 1 in 100 to 1 in 190 in hilly areas. However on curves grade compensation is provided to maintain the speed. Grade compensation provided is 0.04 % on B.G. 0.03 % on M.G. 0.02 % on N.G. (ii) Momentum gradient When there is a change from falling gradient to rising gradient, falling gradient may be slightly increased to make use of momentum gained to climb rising gradient. This is called momentum of gradient. This stretch of track should not have any signal. (iii) Pusher gradient In ghat section sometimes more gradient is provided. To climb this extra engine may be used. Between Mumbai and Poona a gradient of 1 in 37 is also provided. This is known as pusher gradient. (iv) Station yard gradient At railway stations and yards for easy drainage gradients provided are a maximum of 1 in 400 and a minimum of 1 in 1000. Superelevation On curves superelevation required is S = where V = speed in m/sec, G = gauge in metre R = radius in metre and g = 9.81 m/sec2. Maximum superelevation on broad gauge works out to be For V = 100 kmph 140 mm V = 120 kmph 165 mm V = 160 kmph 185 mm Cant deficiency It is the difference between necessary cant for design speed and actual cant. question: Traffic volume, its directional distribution and its composition. The traffic volume is the number of vehicles moving in a specified direction on a roadway that passes a given point during specified unit of time. The design of a highway should be based on the factual data about traffic volume, its directional distribution and its composition. The most important items in the study of traffic volume are average daily traffic, peak hour traffic and the traffic projection factor. The Average Daily Traffic  (A.D.T.) is defined as the average number of vehicles per day passing on a section of the road during a particular year. For design purposes, a reasonable hourly volume (design hourly volume or D.H.V.) which represents a volume higher than the volume during most of the hours in a year, should be taken. Highway Engineering In order to take into consideration the future needs of traffic over a period of 20–25 years, which is taken as the normal life of a highway, it is essential to know the development of traffic and its ultimate volume at the end of the design period. Though it is not possible to project the traffic to some future year exactly, yet a reasonably exact value can be arrived by considering the following elements:

options.a: Current traffic, made up of existing traffic and diverted traffic.
options.b: Increase in traffic due to normal traffic growth, generated traffic and development traffic.

question: For cast-in-situ piles, the minimum reinforcement should be 0.40% of the sectional area calculated on the basis of outside area of the casing or shaft. The reinforcement can be curtailed along the depth of pile depending upon the nature of soil. In soft clay or loose sands, the reinforcement is to be provided upto the full pile depth. The minimum clear distance between two main reinforcements shall be 100 mm. For rigid/moment resistant connections, double plated framed connections or double angle framed connections may be used. Design of Plate Girders * Plate girder is a built up I-section beam with two flange plates connected to a web plate of required depth. * A plate girders need stiffeners to avoid buckling of web. question: Which of the following is a sedimentary rock?
explanation: Sandstone is formed by the deposition of sand particles in layers, making it a sedimentary rock.
subject: Building Materials & Construction Practices

question: Under metamorphism, Limestone changes into:
explanation: Metamorphism of calcareous rocks like limestone results in the formation of marble.
subject: Building Materials & Construction Practices

question: Granite is chemically classified as a:
explanation: Granite is composed primarily of silica (quartz and feldspar), making it a siliceous rock.
subject: Building Materials & Construction Practices

explanation: The Mohs scale characterizes the scratch resistance of minerals through the ability of a harder material to scratch a softer material.

question: Which branch of Engineering Mechanics specifically analyzes deformations in actual structures as part of its core study, unlike the branch that neglects them?
explanation: Engineering Mechanics is divided into three parts. In Mechanics of Rigid body, deformation is assumed very small and neglected. However, in Mechanics of Deformable body (also called Strength of Materials), deformations in actual structures are analyzed.
subject: Engineering Mechanics & Strength of Materials

question: Consider the following statements regarding the subdivisions of rigid body mechanics: 1. Statics deals with the study of a body at a rest condition, exemplified by the static design of a building. 2. Dynamics is the study of bodies in motion, such as the earthquake analysis of a building. 3. Kinematics is the study of a body in motion with considering the external forces that cause the motion. Which of the above statements are correct?
explanation: Statics is the study of bodies at rest, and Dynamics is the study of bodies in motion. Dynamics is further split into Kinematics and Kinetics. Kinematics studies motion without considering the forces causing it, while Kinetics considers the forces causing the motion. Therefore, statement 3 is incorrect because it describes Kinetics.
subject: Engineering Mechanics & Strength of Materials

question: Which of the following forces is classified as a body force that acts through the total volume of the object rather than just its contact area?
explanation: Forces are classified as body forces or surface forces. Body forces act through the total volume and include gravitation, electrostatic, magnetic, and inertia forces. Surface forces act through the surface and include friction, surface tension, and pressure.
subject: Engineering Mechanics & Strength of Materials

question:
Assertion (A): Mass is an absolute quantity that does not vary with respect to any place or time.
Reason (R): Weight is a force with which a body is attracted towards the center of the earth and varies based on the local acceleration due to gravity g.

options.a: Both A and R are true and R is the correct explanation of A.
options.b: Both A and R are true but R is not the correct explanation of A.
options.c: A is true but R is false.
options.d: A is false but R is true.

explanation: Both statements are individually true. Mass represents the quantity of matter and is constant everywhere. Weight is a pulling force defined by W = m × g, and since g varies by location, weight also varies. However, the variation of weight is not the cause or explanation for why mass remains constant; they are independent physical definitions.
subject: Engineering Mechanics & Strength of Materials

question: According to Newton’s second law of motion, the magnitude of force required to provide an acceleration of 1 m/sec² to a mass of 1 kg is defined as:
explanation: Based on Newton's second law, Force F = mass × acc. One Newton force is defined as that which gives an acceleration of 1 m/sec² to a body of mass 1 kg. Mathematically, 1 N = 1 kg × 1 m/sec² = 1 kg·m/sec².
subject: Engineering Mechanics & Strength of Materials

question:
Match the following.
List I A. Magnitude B. Direction C. Sense D. Point of application   List II 1. Push or pull 2. Line of action 3. 10 kN 4. Exact location at point C
explanation: The characteristics (elements) of a force include Magnitude (e.g., 10 kN), Direction or line of action (e.g., vertically downward), Sense or nature (push or pull), and Point of application (e.g., at point C).
subject: Engineering Mechanics & Strength of Materials

question: Consider the following statements regarding the effects and characteristics of a force: 1. A force may change the motion of a body or retard existing forces to bring a body to rest. 2. The Principle of Transmissibility allows a force to be considered at any point on its line of action on a rigid body. 3. A force acting on a body can never give rise to internal stresses. Which of the above statements are correct?
explanation: Forces can change motion or bring a body to rest (equilibrium). The Principle of Transmissibility states a force can be assumed to act anywhere on its line of action. Statement 3 is incorrect because forces can indeed give rise to internal stresses in the body on which they act.
subject: Engineering Mechanics & Strength of Materials

question:
Assertion (A): If a force acts at any point on a rigid body, its effect on the body's state of rest or motion remains unchanged if it is shifted to any other point on its line of action.
Reason (R): This is known as the Principle of Transmissibility, where the force is assumed to act on its time of action.

options.a: Both A and R are true and R is the correct explanation of A.
options.b: Both A and R are true but R is not the correct explanation of A.
options.c: A is true but R is false.
options.d: A is false but R is true.

explanation: The Principle of Transmissibility specifies that a force acting at any point on a rigid body may be considered to act at any other point on its line of action without changing the effect. The Reason correctly defines this principle as stated in the notes.
subject: Engineering Mechanics & Strength of Materials

question: Which force system is characterized by all lines of action of the constituent forces intersecting at a single common point?
explanation: According to the classification of systems of forces, concurrent forces are those whose lines of action intersect at a common point. If they also lie on the same plane, they are coplanar concurrent forces.
subject: Engineering Mechanics & Strength of Materials

question:
A force F acts on a body at an angle θ relative to the horizontal plane as shown below. Identify the correct horizontal (Fₓ) and vertical (Fᵧ) components.

       ^ Y
       |   / F
       |  /
       | / θ
       |/______|____> X

options.a: Fₓ = F sin θ, Fᵧ = F cos θ
options.b: Fₓ = F cos θ, Fᵧ = F sin θ
options.c: Fₓ = F tan θ, Fᵧ = F sin θ
options.d: Fₓ = F sec θ, Fᵧ = F cosec θ

explanation: The resolution of forces involves replacing a force F by two forces along the x and y axes. When the force makes an angle θ with the horizontal, the horizontal component is Fₓ = F cos θ and the vertical component is Fᵧ = F sin θ.
subject: Engineering Mechanics & Strength of Materials

question: For a body of weight W resting on a plane inclined at an angle θ to the horizontal, what is the component of weight acting normal (perpendicular) to the inclined plane?
explanation: When resolving weight W on an inclined plane (Resolution Case 2), the component parallel to the inclined plane is Wₚ = W sin θ, and the component normal (perpendicular) to the plane is Wₙ = W cos θ.
subject: Engineering Mechanics & Strength of Materials

question: Identify the law of forces used to find the resultant of two concurrent forces where the resultant is represented by the diagonal of a parallelogram formed by the two force vectors.
explanation: The parallelogram law states that if two forces F₁ and F₂ acting at a point are represented in magnitude and direction by two sides of a parallelogram, their resultant is represented by the diagonal of that parallelogram in both magnitude and direction.
subject: Engineering Mechanics & Strength of Materials

question: In the composition of two forces F₁ and F₂ acting at an angle θ, the magnitude of the resultant R is found to be minimum when the angle θ between the forces is:
explanation: The magnitude of the resultant is R = √[F₁² + 2F₁F₂ cos θ + F₂²]. R is minimum when cos θ is at its minimum value (-1), which occurs at θ = 180°. In this case, Rₘᵢₙ = |F₁ - F₂|.
subject: Engineering Mechanics & Strength of Materials

question: Consider the following statements regarding laws of forces: 1. The Triangle law states that if two forces are represented by two sides of a triangle in order, the third side in opposite order represents the resultant. 2. If three forces acting on a body are represented by three sides of a triangle in order, the body will be in equilibrium. 3. The Polygon law is used to find the resultant of a number of non-coplanar parallel forces. Which of the above statements are correct?
explanation: Statements 1 and 2 accurately describe the Triangle Law of forces. Statement 3 is incorrect because the Polygon Law is specifically defined for a number of coplanar concurrent forces.
subject: Engineering Mechanics & Strength of Materials

question:
Assertion (A): A particle moving at a constant terminal velocity is technically considered to be in a state of equilibrium.
Reason (R): For a system to be in equilibrium, the change in velocity must be zero, resulting in a zero acceleration and therefore a zero resultant force ∑F = 0.

options.a: Both A and R are true and R is the correct explanation of A.
options.b: Both A and R are true but R is not the correct explanation of A.
options.c: A is true but R is false.
options.d: A is false but R is true.

explanation: Equilibrium represents not only a particle at rest but also one moving at constant or terminal velocity. In such cases, acceleration is mass × 0 = 0, which means the resultant force acting on the system must be equal to zero.
subject: Engineering Mechanics & Strength of Materials

question: According to the Three force principle of equilibrium, if a body is acted upon by three forces and remains in equilibrium, then:

options.a: All three forces must be parallel.
options.b: The resultant of any two forces must be equal, opposite, and collinear with the third force.
options.c: The sum of any two forces must be equal to the third force.
options.d: The forces must form an equilateral triangle.

explanation: The Three force principle states that if a body in equilibrium is acted upon by three forces, then the resultant of any two forces must be equal, opposite, and collinear with the third force.
subject: Engineering Mechanics & Strength of Materials

question:
Match the following.
List I A. Two force principle B. Three force principle C. Four force principle D. Non-concurrent equilibrium   List II 1. Resultant of two pairs must balance 2. Equal, opposite, and collinear 3. ∑V = 0, ∑H = 0, ∑M = 0 4. Resultant of two balances the third
explanation: Two force principle: forces must be equal, opposite, and collinear. Three force principle: resultant of two balances the third. Four force principle: resultant of two balances the resultant of the other two. Non-concurrent equilibrium requires translational and rotational sums to be zero.
subject: Engineering Mechanics & Strength of Materials

question: Lami’s Theorem is a specific application of which mathematical rule for three co-planar forces acting at a point in equilibrium?
explanation: Lami’s Theorem states that if three co-planar forces acting at a point be in equilibrium, then each force is proportional to the sine of the angle between the other two. This is explicitly noted as a "sine rule" application.
subject: Engineering Mechanics & Strength of Materials

question: Which of the following is an essential requirement for a sketch to be technically defined as a Free Body Diagram (FBD)?

options.a: The body must be shown with all its original supports and adjacent structures.
options.b: The body must be isolated from surroundings and show all external forces, internal forces, and moments.
options.c: The diagram must only show the self-weight acting at the centroi
options.d: (d) The body must be in a state of constant acceleration.

explanation: A Free Body Diagram is a sketch where a body has been isolated from surrounding bodies. It must show all external forces, internal forces, and moments acting on the body while it is maintained under equilibrium conditions.
subject: Engineering Mechanics & Strength of Materials

question:
Study the Free Body Diagram of the roller resting against a block below. What does the vector labeled R represent?

      P (Pulling force)
       \
        \   / R
         ( O )
        / | \
       N  W  (Block)

options.a: The normal reaction from the ground
options.c: The reaction force from the block

explanation: In the Free Body Diagram of a roller resting against a block, P is the pulling force, W is the self-weight, N is the normal reaction from the ground, and R is the reaction force from the block that the roller is pushing against.
subject: Engineering Mechanics & Strength of Materials

question: In a non-concurrent force system, the moment of a force about a specific point A is mathematically defined as:

options.a: Mₐ = F × d (where d is parallel distance)
options.b: Mₐ = F / d (where d is perpendicular distance)
options.c: Mₐ = F × d (where d is perpendicular distance)

explanation: The moment of a force about a point is defined as the product of the force and the perpendicular distance d of the line of action of the force from that point. Mathematically: Mₐ = F × d.
subject: Engineering Mechanics & Strength of Materials

question: Two forces of equal magnitude F and opposite nature separated by a particular perpendicular distance z form a couple. Which of the following is true about its effect?

options.b: It produces both translational and rotational motion.
options.c: Its resultant force is zero, but it produces a rotational effect.
options.d: Its moment varies depending on the point of calculation.

explanation: A couple is formed by two equal and opposite forces at distance z. Since they are equal and opposite, the resultant is zero, meaning no translation occurs. However, they produce a moment M = F × z, which creates rotation.
subject: Engineering Mechanics & Strength of Materials

question: Consider the following statements regarding Varignon’s Theorem: 1. It is also known as the Principle of Moment for non-concurrent forces. 2. It states that the algebraic sum of moments of individual forces about a point is equal to the moment of their resultant about the same point. 3. It is mathematically expressed as W₁x₁ + W₂x₂ + W₃x₃ = R × x̄. Which of the above statements are correct?
explanation: All statements accurately describe Varignon’s Theorem. It equates the sum of individual moments to the moment of the resultant force about the same reference point.
subject: Engineering Mechanics & Strength of Materials

question: For a concurrent force system acting on a rigid body to be in equilibrium, the mandatory mathematical conditions are:
explanation: For a concurrent system, where all forces pass through a single point, the moment about that point is inherently zero. Thus, the body is in equilibrium if the resultant force R is zero. Mathematically, since R = √[(∑V)² + (∑H)²], R is zero only if both ∑V = 0 and ∑H = 0.
subject: Engineering Mechanics & Strength of Materials

question: Arrange the following steps in the correct analytical sequence to find the resultant of a non-concurrent coplanar force system: 1. Find the algebraic sum of all horizontal components (∑H) and vertical components (∑V). 2. Resolve each force into its horizontal (F cos θ) and vertical (F sin θ) components. 3. Calculate the magnitude R = √[(∑H)² + (∑V)²]. 4. Calculate the inclination α = tan⁻¹(∑V/∑H).
explanation: The conversion of a force system into an equivalent single force (composition) requires first resolving each individual force. Next, the algebraic sums of these components are found. These sums are then used to calculate the magnitude of the resultant, and finally, its inclination angle is determined.
subject: Engineering Mechanics & Strength of Materials

question: A brick with its shorter face in the direction of the wall is known as:
explanation: In brick masonry terminology, a brick laid such that its shorter face (width × height) is visible on the face of the wall is called a header. A stretcher has its longer face visible
subject: Building Materials & Construction Practices

question: Perpend is an imaginary line of mortar joints in brick masonry which is:
explanation: To achieve proper bond and avoid continuous vertical joints (which weaken the wall), the vertical joints in alternate courses must align perfectly. This imaginary vertical alignment line is called a perpend.
subject: Building Materials & Construction Practices

question: Portion of the brick cut across full width is known as:
explanation: A bat is a piece of a brick cut across its width (e.g., half-bat or three-quarter bat). Closers are typically cut along the length (longitudinally) to maintain the bond near corners.
subject: Building Materials & Construction Practices

question: Construction of a wall starts from:

options.a: one corner and proceeds to other corner
options.b: both corners and proceeds to middle portion
options.c: middle portion and proceeds towards corners

explanation: To ensure proper alignment, level, and plumbness, brickwork always begins at the extreme ends or corners (quoins). The corners are raised first, and a string is stretched between them to guide the construction of the middle portion.
subject: Building Materials & Construction Practices

question:
Match the equipment/term used in brick manufacturing with its primary purpose,
List I A. Pug mill B. Fillet C. Stock board D. Clamp, List II 1.For burning bricks 2.For table moulding 3.For making frog 4.For tempering.
explanation: A pug mill is used for kneading/tempering the clay. A fillet (or pallet) is placed in the mould to form the frog. A stock board is a base used in table moulding. A clamp is a traditional, intermittent method for burning bricks
subject: Building Materials & Construction Practices

question: Assertion (A): In a brick masonry wall thicker than 1.5 bricks, English bond is preferred over Flemish bond. Reason (R): English bond consists of alternate courses of stretchers and headers, which provides a larger number of headers, making the transverse tying of the wall much stronger.

options.a: Both A and R are true and R is the correct explanation of A.
options.b: Both A and R are true but R is not the correct explanation of A.
options.c: A is true but R is false.
options.d: A is false but R is true.

explanation: English bond is structurally the strongest bond because the continuous alignment of headers in alternate courses effectively ties the face of the wall to the backing. Flemish bond is more aesthetically pleasing but slightly weaker in walls thicker than 1.5 bricks.
subject: Building Materials & Construction Practices

question: Consider the following statements regarding the characteristics of a good building brick: 1.The compressive strength of the brick must be greater than .2.Water absorption should not exceed 20% of its dry weight after 24 hours of soaking.3.It should not show any white patches (efflorescence) when dried after soaking in water. Which of the above statements are correct?
explanation: All statements correctly define the IS code requirements for first-class or good quality bricks. A minimum crushing strength of , water absorption , and zero to slight efflorescence are mandatory standards.
subject: Building Materials & Construction Practices

question: Which of the following constituents, if present in excess, causes the brick to melt and lose its shape during burning?
explanation: While a small amount of lime is necessary to act as a flux (helping silica melt slightly to bind the brick), an excess of lime causes the brick to melt completely, distorting its shape during the burning process.
subject: Building Materials & Construction Practices

question: Assertion (A): Iron pyrites and sulphates of calcium and potassium are highly harmful ingredients in brick earth. Reason (R): Alkalis like sulphates of calcium and potassium absorb moisture from the atmosphere and lead to efflorescence on the brick surface.

options.a: Both A and R are true and R is the correct explanation of A.
options.b: Both A and R are true but R is not the correct explanation of A.
options.c: A is true but R is false.
options.d: A is false but R is true.

explanation: Extracted from source content.
subject: Building Materials & Construction Practices

question: In brick manufacturing, what is the sequence of the "Preparation of Clay" stage?1.Digging 2.Unsoling 3.Weathering 4.Cleaning 5.Tempering 6.Blending .
explanation: The correct sequence is: Unsoling (removing the top 200mm of earth), Digging, Cleaning (removing stones/pebbles), Weathering (leaving it exposed to the atmosphere), Blending (mixing in required ingredients), and finally Tempering (kneading in a pug mill to achieve plasticity).
subject: Building Materials & Construction Practices

question: Diagram-based Visualization: In brick masonry, a wall is temporarily left incomplete at the end of a day's work. It is terminated in a stepped manner rather than a vertical straight line to allow for strong bonding when work resumes. This method of stepping back the wall is called:
explanation: Racking back is the process of stepping back successive courses of masonry. Toothing, by contrast, is leaving alternate courses projecting outward. Racking back is generally preferred for structural stability over toothing.
subject: Building Materials & Construction Practices

question: Match the type of cut bricks (closers/bats) with their descriptions: List I (Type) A. Queen closer B. King closer C. Bevelled closer D. Half Bat List II (Description) 1.A brick cut lengthwise into two equal halves. 2. A brick cut in half across its width. 3.A brick cut diagonally from one corner to the center of one end. 4. A brick with a triangular corner removed (half header and half stretcher).
explanation: A Queen closer is cut longitudinally in half. A King closer has a triangular piece cut off from one corner. A bevelled closer is chamfered. A half-bat is cut crosswise in the middle.
subject: Building Materials & Construction Practices

question: Consider the following statements regarding the field testing of bricks: 1.Two bricks struck together should produce a ringing, metallic sound. 2.A good brick should break easily to reveal a homogeneous internal structure. 3.A good brick should not break when dropped from a height of 1 meter onto hard ground. 4.A fingernail should easily leave a scratch mark on a first-class brick to indicate plasticity. Which of the above statements are correct?
explanation: A good brick should not break easily (Statement 2 is false), and it must be hard enough that a fingernail does not leave a scratch
subject: Building Materials & Construction Practices

question: In brick masonry construction, a wall should not be raised by more than 1.5 meters in a single day. Reason (R): Raising the wall too high while the mortar is still wet and green can cause the lower courses to deform or squeeze out under the weight.

options.a: Both A and R are true and R is the correct explanation of A.
options.b: Both A and R are true but R is not the correct explanation of A.
options.c: A is true but R is false.
options.d: A is false but R is true.

explanation: Mortar takes time to set. If excessive weight is placed on fresh mortar joints, they will compress, altering the plumb line and stability of the wall. Therefore, a 1.5 m maximum height restriction per day is enforced.
subject: Building Materials & Construction Practices

question: Which type of kiln is most commonly used for large-scale, continuous manufacturing of bricks where the firing zone moves around a circular or oval track while the bricks remain stationary?
explanation: Hoffman's kiln is a continuous kiln built above ground (circular or oval) where the fire is moved from chamber to chamber through the stationary stacked bricks. Bull's trench is partially underground. In a Tunnel kiln, the fire is stationary, and bricks move on cars.
subject: Building Materials & Construction Practices

question: In a good brick earth, the ideal percentage of Silica is:
explanation: Silica is the major constituent of brick earth (50-60%). It prevents cracking, shrinking, and warping of bricks. Alumina is 20-30%, and Iron Oxide is 5-6%.
subject: Building Materials & Construction Practices

question: Match the following special types of bricks to their primary purpose: List I A. Paving Bricks B. Fire Clay Bricks C. Perforated Bricks D. Facing Bricks List II 1.High aesthetic finish for exteriors without plaster. 2.Lightweight construction and thermal insulation. 3.Resisting extremely high temperatures in furnaces. 4.Resisting heavy abrasion on street floors.
explanation: Paving bricks are vitrified for abrasion resistance (street floors). Fire clay bricks (refractory) resist high heat. Perforated bricks reduce weight and insulate. Facing bricks are uniform in color/shape for exposed aesthetic walls.
subject: Building Materials & Construction Practices

question: What is the function of providing a "Frog" in a brick?

options.a: To reduce the overall weight of the brick.
options.b: To provide a shear key for the mortar to interlock with the successive course.
options.c: To aid in the drying process before burning.
options.d: To indicate the manufacturer's name only.

explanation: While the frog may contain the manufacturer's name and slightly reduce weight, its primary structural function is to form a mechanical key (shear key) for the mortar, binding the courses together. It is typically kept facing upwards during masonry work.
subject: Building Materials & Construction Practices

question: Which of the following bonds consists of alternate courses of headers and stretchers?
explanation: In an English bond, one course consists entirely of stretchers, and the next course consists entirely of headers. In a Flemish bond, headers and stretchers are alternated within the same course.
subject: Building Materials & Construction Practices

question: In the preparation of brick earth, blending is done immediately after digging the soil. Reason (R): Weathering must precede blending to allow the soil to soften and mature under atmospheric action.

options.a: Both A and R are true and R is the correct explanation of A.
options.b: Both A and R are true but R is not the correct explanation of A.
options.c: A is true but R is false.
options.d: A is false but R is true.

explanation: Blending is NOT done immediately after digging. The soil must first undergo weathering (exposure to atmosphere for weeks) to soften. Only after weathering is the clay blended with other required ingredients.
subject: Building Materials & Construction Practices

question: Which ingredient in brick earth imparts the characteristic red color to the burnt brick?
explanation: Iron oxide (present in 5% to 6%) acts as a flux and imparts the red color to bricks upon burning. If it is present in excess, the bricks become dark blue or blackish.
subject: Building Materials & Construction Practices

question: Which of the following describes a "Bull's Trench Kiln"?

options.a: An intermittent kiln where bricks are burnt in batches and must cool completely before the next batch.
options.b: A continuous, partially underground oval trench where the firing zone moves through stationary bricks.
options.c: A continuous kiln where bricks are moved on trolleys through a stationary firing zone.
options.d: A temporary structure constructed entirely of raw bricks and fuel layers above ground.

explanation: Bull's trench kiln is a continuous kiln constructed in a trench (underground/semi-underground). The fire moves around the oval trench while the stacked bricks remain stationary. A clamp (d) is temporary and intermittent. A tunnel kiln (c) has moving bricks.
subject: Building Materials & Construction Practices

question: To check the size of brick number of bricks to be kept side by side is:
explanation: In standard laboratory shape and size tests for bricks, 20 bricks are lined up side by side to measure variations and tolerances
subject: Building Materials & Construction Practices

question: Depressions provided in face of brick during moulding are:
explanation: The frog is the depression on the top face of the brick which serves as a shear key to hold the mortar for the successive course.
subject: Building Materials & Construction Practices

question: The brick is considered dry when the moisture content is:
explanation: During the drying stage before burning, a brick is considered adequately dry when its internal moisture content drops to 2%
subject: Building Materials & Construction Practices
